text: A magazine stores two types of Hazard Division 1.3 propellants from different manufacturers. A safety review confirmed that storing them together does not significantly increase either the probability of an accident or, for a given quantity, the magnitude of the effects. Per AASTP-1 para 1.2.2.1, they are therefore considered compatible and may be stored together.

A storage building houses both HD 1.2 ammunition and a stock of highly flammable cleaning solvents on adjacent shelves. The depot commander argued that the quantity of solvent is small. Per AASTP-1 para 1.2.2.1, ammunition and explosives should not be stored together with goods that can hazard them, including highly flammable materials, regardless of quantity.

An explosives store holds HD 1.3 propellants alongside barrels of concentrated acid used for equipment maintenance. The storage officer noted that the acid is sealed. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1 explicitly lists acids and corrosives as goods that should not be stored with ammunition and explosives; the arrangement is non-compliant.

A depot stores HD 1.1 and HD 1.4 ammunition in the same igloo. AASTP-1 Note 1 to the mixing table states that HD 1.4 may be stored with any other hazard division without aggregation of the NEQ. Storing HD 1.4 with HD 1.1 in this manner is explicitly permitted.

A magazine holds a mixture of HD 1.2.1 shaped charges and HD 1.3 propellants. The NEQ was aggregated as HD 1.3. AASTP-1 Note 2 states that when 1.2 shaped charges are present in a 1.2.1/1.2.2 and 1.3 mix, the mix must be aggregated as HD 1.1 unless relevant trials or analysis indicate otherwise. Aggregating as HD 1.3 is non-compliant.

A storage facility holds mixed HD 1.2.1/1.2.2 and HD 1.3 propellants in standard packaging with no shaped charges, no high-energy propellants, normal loading density, and all individual articles having NEQ ≤ 5 kg. The aggregate was treated as HD 1.2 or 1.3. Per AASTP-1 Note 2, none of the four triggering conditions are present; the assessment is compliant.

A depot stores HD 1.2.1 articles with an individual NEQ of 8 kg each mixed with HD 1.3 material. The aggregate was treated as HD 1.3. AASTP-1 Note 2(d) states that 1.2.1 articles with individual NEQ > 5 kg require the mix to be aggregated as HD 1.1. Treating the aggregate as HD 1.3 is non-compliant.

Tank gun propellants classified as HD 1.3 (high-energy propellants) are stored together with HD 1.2.2 items. The mix NEQ was aggregated as HD 1.2. AASTP-1 Note 2(b) lists high-energy propellants as a condition requiring aggregation as HD 1.1. Using HD 1.2 aggregation is non-compliant.

A small quantity of HD 1.1 demolition explosives is stored in a large magazine predominantly holding HD 1.2.1/1.2.2 ammunition, arranged so the mixture behaves as HD 1.2.1/1.2.2. AASTP-1 para 1.2.3.3(b) states it should be possible to arrange such storage so the mixture behaves as 1.2.1/1.2.2. The arrangement is compliant.

A National Competent Authority granted permission to modify the standard mixing rules for a specific depot based on adequately documented technical justification and tests. AASTP-1 para 1.2.3.3(a) explicitly allows national competent authorities to modify mixing rules subject to adequate technical justification. The modification is compliant.

A depot commander modified the mixing rules for HD 1.1 and HD 1.3 storage without obtaining National Competent Authority approval, citing operational necessity. AASTP-1 1.2.3.3(a) requires National Competent Authority approval for any modification to mixing rules. Unilateral modification by a depot commander is non-compliant.

A magazine holds a mixed store of HD 1.1, HD 1.2.1 and HD 1.3. The QD applied is the greatest value obtained when considering the aggregate NEQ as each of HD 1.1, 1.2.1, 1.2.2, and 1.3 separately. AASTP-1 para 1.2.3.3(c) requires exactly this approach for mixed HD 1.1/1.2.1/1.2.2/1.3 storage. The QD selection is compliant.

A mixed store of HD 1.1, HD 1.2.1 and HD 1.3 applies a QD based on the HD 1.3 aggregate only, since HD 1.3 represents the largest mass. AASTP-1 1.2.3.3(c) requires the greatest QD across all four possible aggregations. Using only the HD 1.3 aggregate ignores the possibility of higher QD from HD 1.1 aggregation; non-compliant.

Each type of ammunition in a depot is stored in a separate magazine. The safety officer noted this exceeds the minimum requirement. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1(c) states that safety of ammunition and explosives would be enhanced if each kind was kept separate. Separate storage is the preferred and compliant approach.

HD 1.3 propellants are stored in conditions of high loading density in heavily confined shelving. The mix with HD 1.2.2 is aggregated as HD 1.3. AASTP-1 Note 2(c) requires aggregation as HD 1.1 for high loading density storage of 1.3 in relatively heavy confinement. Aggregating as HD 1.3 is non-compliant.

An operational requirement necessitates storing HD 1.2 and HD 1.3 ammunition in the same building to reduce logistic delays. A safety analysis confirmed the mixed storage does not significantly increase the probability or magnitude of an accident. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1(c) acknowledges that a balance of safety interests against other factors may require mixing; the arrangement with documented analysis is compliant.

A workshop stores HD 1.4 cartridges alongside corrosive battery acid in the same room to save space. The officer in charge argued HD 1.4 presents no significant hazard. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1(b) prohibits storing ammunition with goods that can hazard them, including corrosives, regardless of the hazard division of the ammunition. Non-compliant.

Two PES stores contain a mixed HD 1.2.2 and HD 1.4 load. The NEQ is not aggregated because HD 1.4 may be stored with any other HD without NEQ aggregation. AASTP-1 Note 1 explicitly states HD 1.4 may be stored with any other HD without aggregation. The NEQ treatment is compliant.

A mixed store contains HD 1.2 shaped charges and HD 1.3 ammunition. The safety officer noted that similar mixtures at other depots have been treated as HD 1.3 without incident. AASTP-1 Note 2(a) requires aggregation as HD 1.1 when 1.2 shaped charges are present, unless specific trials indicate otherwise. Historical precedent does not substitute for trials; non-compliant.

A compatibility assessment for a new store concluded that HD 1.1 demolition charges and HD 1.2 ammunition may not be stored together in the same igloo because the combination would significantly increase the magnitude of an accident. Separate igloos are assigned. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1(a) defines incompatible as significantly increasing probability or magnitude; the assessment leading to separation is a compliant action.

For a mixed HD 1.1, 1.2.1 and 1.3 situation, the QD computed assuming the aggregate is 100% HD 1.1 gives 450 m; assuming HD 1.2.1 gives 280 m; assuming HD 1.3 gives 200 m. The depot applies 450 m. Per AASTP-1 1.2.3.3(c), the greatest QD across all scenarios must be applied; 450 m is correct.

For a mixed HD 1.1/1.2.1/1.3 situation, the depot applies the QD corresponding to HD 1.2.1 (280 m) because HD 1.2.1 is the largest mass component. AASTP-1 1.2.3.3(c) requires the greatest QD across all aggregation options; the HD 1.1 aggregation gives 450 m. Applying 280 m is non-compliant.

A storage building houses HD 1.3 propellants and large quantities of wooden packing crates considered 'store furnishings.' A safety review noted the crates are not themselves explosive. AASTP-1 1.2.2.1(b) prohibits co-storage with highly flammable materials; large quantities of dry wood are highly flammable and could significantly increase accident magnitude. Non-compliant.

A depot holds HD 1.2.1 and HD 1.3 materials with no shaped charges, no high-energy propellants, normal loading density, and individual HD 1.2.1 NEQ ≤ 3 kg. None of the four conditions in AASTP-1 Note 2 that require HD 1.1 aggregation are met. Treating the mix as HD 1.2 or 1.3 is compliant.

A depot commander wants to store HD 1.1 and HD 1.2 together. A formal compatibility assessment is conducted per AASTP-1 1.2.2.1, confirming the combination does not significantly increase accident probability or effect magnitude. The mixed storage proceeds with documented justification. The process of conducting a documented assessment before proceeding is compliant.

A PES contains 5,000 kg of HD 1.2 ammunition. The full 5,000 kg is used as the NEQ for quantity-distance computations. AASTP-1 para 1.3.2.3(c) states the total explosives content of rounds classified HD 1.2 is used for NEQ computation. The approach is compliant.

Two PES stores each hold 3,000 kg NEQ and are separated by only 80 m, which is less than the required Inter-Magazine Distance. The Q-D computation was performed separately for each store at 3,000 kg. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) states PES not separated by the appropriate IMD must be treated as a single site; the aggregate 6,000 kg must be used. Non-compliant.

Two PES stores, each holding 4,000 kg NEQ, are separated by a distance exceeding the required Inter-Magazine Distance. Q-D is computed separately for each using 4,000 kg. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) allows separate NEQ computation when PES are properly separated by the IMD. The computation is compliant.

Three PES each holding 2,000 kg are treated as three separate sites for Q-D purposes, but none are separated by the required IMD from the others. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) requires all non-IMD-separated PES to be treated as a single site with aggregate NEQ of 6,000 kg. The approach of using 2,000 kg per site is non-compliant.

The total NEQ of ammunition in a single PES is 8,500 kg. A special assessment determined that the effective explosive yield is not significantly different from the actual NEQ. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(a) uses total NEQ unless determined that the effective quantity is significantly different. Using 8,500 kg is compliant.

An HD 1.1 PES holds 10,000 kg of packaged military explosives. No special circumstances apply that would make the effective quantity significantly different. The full 10,000 kg NEQ is used for Q-D. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(a) requires total NEQ; this is compliant.

A test site stores 3,000 kg NEQ of bulk demolition explosives (devoid of metal casing or components) in fibreboard packaging, not on pallets, in open storage or igloos. An IBD less than the standard HD 1.1 distance is applied. AASTP-1 para 1.3.2.3/IBD note allows reduced IBD for NEQ < 4,500 kg when hazardous projections cannot arise; this situation qualifies. Compliant.

A PES contains 3,500 kg NEQ of bulk demolition explosives in steel-cased artillery shells. An IBD less than the standard HD 1.1 table distance is applied, citing NEQ < 4,500 kg. AASTP-1's reduced IBD provision for NEQ < 4,500 kg applies only when hazardous projections cannot arise — steel-cased shells produce hazardous projections. Non-compliant.

A standard military ammunition depot stores 4,000 kg NEQ of boxed HD 1.1 artillery shells on pallets. The depot applies a reduced IBD citing NEQ < 4,500 kg. The reduced IBD provision in AASTP-1 applies only to bulk demolition explosives devoid of metal components in fibreboard packaging. Palletised ammunition does not qualify. Non-compliant.

A factory stores 2,000 kg NEQ of bulk sheet explosive (devoid of metal) in fibreboard boxes, not on pallets, in a special igloo. A reduced IBD is applied. AASTP-1 permits reduced IBD for NEQ < 4,500 kg when the explosive is devoid of metal casing, in fibreboard packaging, not palletised, in open storage or igloos. Compliant.

A PES holds a single type of HD 1.1 explosive. The NEQ is determined as the total mass of explosive material present: 7,200 kg. This figure is used directly for Q-D computation. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(a) states total NEQ of a single PES is used unless the effective quantity is significantly different. Using 7,200 kg is compliant.

A site plan shows four PES, each with 1,500 kg NEQ. None are separated by IMD from adjacent stores. Q-D was computed using 1,500 kg (single store basis) to keep distances manageable. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) requires the aggregate 6,000 kg to be used when stores are not separated by IMD. Using 1,500 kg significantly under-represents the hazard. Non-compliant.

Two adjacent PES each contain 5,000 kg NEQ and are separated by a distance equal to the required IMD. Q-D for external hazards is computed using 5,000 kg each. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) permits treating properly separated PES independently. Compliant.

A Q-D assessment for an HD 1.2 PES uses only the propellant mass, excluding the inert components of the rounds. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(c) requires the total explosives content of rounds classified HD 1.2 — not just propellant — to be used for NEQ. Non-compliant.

A test site with NEQ < 4,500 kg uses bulk demolition explosives in fibreboard packaging without pallets or metal components. The IBD is set at less than the standard Table 1 value. The standard's special provision for factory/test site circumstances with low NEQ and no projection hazard explicitly permits this. Compliant.

Two HD 1.1 PES stores are co-located without IMD separation. Their NEQs (4,000 kg and 3,500 kg) are aggregated to 7,500 kg for all external Q-D computations. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) explicitly requires treating such stores as a single site; using the aggregate 7,500 kg is compliant.

An igloo stores 5,000 kg NEQ of HD 1.1 packaged military explosives in standard wooden ammunition boxes. The depot applies a reduced IBD, citing the provision for NEQ < 4,500 kg. The provision does not apply because (a) NEQ exceeds 4,500 kg and (b) wooden box packaging with metal components does not meet the no-projection-hazard criterion. Non-compliant.

A site plan shows two PES: one with 6,000 kg and one with 4,000 kg, separated by 60 m. The required IMD for 6,000 kg is 90 m. Q-D to inhabited buildings was computed using 6,000 kg (largest store). AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) requires the aggregate 10,000 kg for non-IMD separated stores. Computing from 6,000 kg alone is non-compliant.

A safety assessment confirmed that the effective explosive yield of a specific ammunition type is significantly less than its total NEQ due to its packaging and design. A Q-D based on the lower effective quantity is used with appropriate authority approval. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(a) permits using effective quantity when it is determined to be significantly different from actual NEQ. With proper justification, this approach is compliant.

A depot reduces Q-D by computing NEQ from only the primary explosive component of HD 1.1 items, excluding boosters, igniting charges and auxiliary charges. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3 requires the total NEQ; excluding subsidiary explosive elements without authorised effective-quantity determination is non-compliant.

A depot applies reduced IBD to 4,200 kg NEQ of bulk demolition explosive stored on wooden pallets. The provision for reduced IBD (NEQ < 4,500 kg, no projection hazard) explicitly excludes explosives on pallets. Palletised storage is excluded regardless of NEQ. Non-compliant.

Three PES stores each holding 2,000 kg NEQ are arranged so that each pair is separated by the required IMD. Q-D for external hazards is computed using 2,000 kg per store. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(b) requires aggregate NEQ only for stores not separated by IMD; properly separated stores may be assessed independently. Compliant.

A Q-D plan for an HD 1.1 store uses 75% of the actual NEQ on the basis that not all rounds are expected to detonate simultaneously. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3(a) requires total NEQ unless a formal determination confirms the effective quantity is significantly different. Using an unsupported 75% factor is non-compliant.

A PES holds mixed HD 1.1 and HD 1.4. The total NEQ includes both HD 1.1 and HD 1.4 for Q-D computation. AASTP-1 1.3.2.3 requires total NEQ; since HD 1.4 is stored without NEQ aggregation concerns (per Note 1), but the full explosive mass still counts toward the overall site NEQ. Using total mass is compliant.

A factory stores 4,000 kg NEQ of cast TNT blocks devoid of metal in fibreboard packaging, not palletised, in open storage. The IBD is below the standard Table 1 value. AASTP-1's provision permits reduced IBD for NEQ < 4,500 kg when hazardous projections cannot arise; bulk cast TNT in fibreboard with no metal qualifies. Compliant.

A depot plans to site a second ammunition store 200 m from an existing PES. The design engineer confirms 200 m meets or exceeds the minimum Inter-Magazine Distance required by AASTP-1 Annex A Table 1 for the applicable NEQ. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 defines IMD as the minimum permissible distance between PES and storage sites; meeting the minimum is compliant.

A new ammunition store is built 120 m from an existing PES. The required IMD for the given NEQ is 150 m. The site plan notes that the new store is not a potential explosion site itself. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 defines IMD as the minimum between PES and any storage site containing ammunition or explosives; the 120 m distance is below the required 150 m. Non-compliant.

The degree of protection afforded by an Inter-Magazine Distance is reviewed and found to be dependent on the type of ammunition, its packaging, and the construction of the building at the PES. All these factors were considered in determining the required distance. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 states these factors are determinants of IMD protection level. The multi-factor assessment is compliant.

An IMD assessment uses only the NEQ to determine the required distance, ignoring the type of building construction at the PES. The building is an untraversed light structure. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 states that IMD protection is highly dependent on, among other factors, the type and construction of the building at the PES. Ignoring building type is non-compliant.

An HD 1.3 magazine is sited at the Level 1 IMD from an adjacent ES store. The safety assessment confirms virtually complete protection against propagation by flame, radiant heat, firebrands, projections and lobbed ammunition from the PES. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 1 provides exactly this protection. Compliant.

An HD 1.3 ES is sited at the Level 2 IMD from a PES. The design documentation acknowledges a considerable risk that some effects (flame, heat, firebrands, projections) could propagate to the ES contents if effective fire-fighting cannot be promptly deployed. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 2 description matches this; the design is correctly characterised. Compliant.

An HD 1.3 store is sited at the Level 2 IMD distance. The safety plan states that Level 2 provides virtually complete protection against propagation. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 1 provides virtually complete protection; Level 2 provides only a high degree of protection against immediate propagation, not virtually complete. The characterisation is incorrect and the protection may be over-stated. Non-compliant.

IMD calculations consider the sensitiveness of the explosives, the type of packaging and the strength of construction at the PES. The IMD for a sensitive primary explosive is set by individual assessment rather than the standard table. AASTP-1 1.3.1.4 notes primary explosives require individual assessment; applying individual assessment here is compliant.

A standard table IMD is applied to packages of primary explosives. AASTP-1 1.3.1.4 states Inter-Magazine Distances should not be used for packages of primary explosives and other very sensitive explosive substances like blasting gelatine, which require individual assessment. Using standard table IMD is non-compliant.

An HD 1.3 PES and an ES are separated by the Level 1 IMD. A post-incident review confirms that even if fire-fighting cannot be deployed, ignition of combustible building parts would not spread to the ES ammunition contents. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 1 describes exactly this protection level. Compliant.

An HD 1.3 depot applies only the Level 2 IMD for all its magazine pairs, stating that Level 2 is sufficient since fire-fighting will always be available. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 2 provides a high but not virtually complete degree of protection and acknowledges that fire-fighting services may not always be effective. Relying solely on fire-fighting availability to justify Level 2 is non-compliant.

Two HD 1.1 stores are sited such that the distance between them equals the IMD specified in Annex A, Table 1. The packaging is standard NATO wooden boxes and the PES is a standard igloo. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 specifies IMD as minimum permissible; exactly meeting the minimum while meeting all other conditions is compliant.

An ammunition depot relocates a store to 80 m from an existing PES. The required IMD is 100 m. The site plan justifies the 80 m distance by noting the new store has thick walls. AASTP-1 does not permit unilateral reduction of IMD based on the ES wall thickness; reduced distances are only available through barricade provisions. Non-compliant.

An HD 1.3 ES at Level 2 IMD distance from a PES has documented fire-fighting arrangements that acknowledge the risk of propagation if fire-fighting fails. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 2 acknowledges this risk and requires it to be accepted; documenting the acceptance and risk is compliant.

A safety plan for an HD 1.3 depot claims that using Level 2 IMD carries 'no risk of propagation to adjacent stores.' AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 2 explicitly states there is a 'considerable risk' of propagation at this distance. Claiming no risk at Level 2 IMD misrepresents the standard. Non-compliant.

The IMD between two HD 1.1 PES is set at a value appropriate for the level of protection required for the ES, considering the sensitiveness of the explosive, type of ammunition, type of packaging, and building construction. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 lists these exact factors. The assessment is compliant.

A depot applies the same IMD regardless of building type — using the same distance for an open stack as for a heavily reinforced igloo at the ES. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 specifies that the degree of protection is highly dependent on the type and construction of buildings at the PES or ES. Different building types require different IMD. Non-compliant.

An HD 1.3 store pair is sited at Level 1 IMD. The store construction uses non-combustible materials throughout to further reduce propagation risk. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 1 acknowledges combustible building parts may ignite but notes this is unlikely to spread to contents; using non-combustible construction exceeds this standard. Compliant.

A depot's safety submission states that because the ES stores only HD 1.4 items, no IMD is required between it and the adjacent PES (HD 1.1). AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 defines IMD as the distance to protect ammunition at the ES; protecting HD 1.4 at the ES from a HD 1.1 explosion still requires appropriate IMD. Eliminating IMD is non-compliant.

An IMD assessment for primary explosives (blasting gelatine) is conducted by individual assessment rather than using Annex A Table distances. AASTP-1 1.3.1.4 explicitly states primary explosives and very sensitive explosive substances require individual assessment for IMD. Compliant.

An HD 1.3 depot applies Level 1 IMD throughout, with fire-fighting resources available but no reliance placed on them to achieve the safety level. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 Level 1 is designed so that protection is achieved even without effective fire-fighting. Not depending on fire-fighting for Level 1 is the correct application. Compliant.

An HD 1.3 depot sited at Level 2 IMD states fire suppression systems make Level 2 equivalent to Level 1. AASTP-1 1.3.1.11 does not equate Level 2 with Level 1 based on fire suppression; the levels are defined by physical distances. Substituting suppression for distance without authority approval is non-compliant.

A depot's IMD plan was reviewed by the National Competent Authority and confirmed to comply with all factors in AASTP-1 1.3.1.8: sensitiveness of explosives, type of ammunition, packaging, and building construction at PES and ES. The plan is approved. Compliant.

An IMD assessment ignores the packaging type of the ammunition, using only NEQ and building type. AASTP-1 1.3.1.8 lists type of packaging as a key factor in determining IMD protection. Omitting packaging type from the assessment is non-compliant.

Unboxed bombs of HD 1.1 are stored in barricaded stacks, separated by effective earth barricades. The bombs are structurally strong, flammable dunnage is minimised, and the stacking height does not exceed 1 m (D1 distances are used). All four conditions of AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 for barricaded stacks are met. Compliant.

Unboxed bombs of HD 1.1 are stored in barricaded stacks using D1 inter-stack distances. The stacks are 1.5 m high to maximise storage density. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 condition 4 requires stacking height not to exceed 1 m when D1 distances are used. The 1.5 m height is non-compliant.

Unboxed HD 1.1 bombs are stored using D1 barricaded stack distances. The bombs are thin-walled and may be crushed by intense air shock. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 condition 2 requires the bombs to be 'relatively strong so as to withstand intense air shock without being crushed.' Thin-walled bombs that may be crushed do not meet this condition. Non-compliant.

HD 1.1 bombs are stored in barricaded stacks with effective earth barricades, the stacking height ≤ 1 m (D1 applied), minimal combustible dunnage, and structurally strong casings. D1 distances up to 30,000 kg NEQ are applied per AASTP-1 1.3.3.1. All conditions satisfied; compliant.

A stack of HD 1.1 bombs contains substantial wooden dunnage and packaging materials retained for re-packing operations. D1 barricaded stack distances are applied. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 condition 3 requires 'minimum of flammable dunnage' to prevent subsequent mass explosion. Substantial dunnage violates this condition. Non-compliant.

Unboxed HD 1.1 bombs with NEQ of 35,000 kg per stack use D1 barricaded distances. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 limits D1 distances to up to 30,000 kg NEQ; for 30,001–120,000 kg, D2 distances must be used. Applying D1 at 35,000 kg is non-compliant.

Unboxed HD 1.1 bombs with 80,000 kg NEQ per stack use D2 barricaded stack distances. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 allows D2 distances for 30,001 to 120,000 kg NEQ. Applying D2 at 80,000 kg is within the specified range. Compliant.

An effective barricade is installed between two HD 1.1 PES, allowing use of reduced Inter-Magazine Distances from Annex A, Table 1 (barricaded column). AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(a) states an effective barricade avoids very large IMD and the reduced values are given in Table 1. Using reduced barricaded distances is compliant.

A depot installs a barricade and applies reduced IMD. However, the barricade investigation in AASTP-1 shows that reduced exterior distances only apply when the barricade attenuates blast by at least a specified amount. The depot did not verify barricade effectiveness. Applying reduced exterior Q-D without verifying barricade effectiveness is non-compliant.

An explosives workshop at a depot is surrounded by a barricade. The barricade provides some protection for personnel in the lee of the barricade from a nearby PES explosion. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(b) confirms that a barricade or heavy wall around an ES workshop provides some protection for personnel behind it. The arrangement is recognised by the standard. Compliant.

A depot reduces exterior IBD from a PES by applying 'barricade credit' even though there is no barricade between the PES and the inhabited building. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(c) confirms blast and projection data from trials shows that barricades reduce exterior Q-D; without an actual barricade, no credit can be applied. Non-compliant.

D2 barricaded stack distances are applied between stacks of 50,000 kg NEQ of HD 1.1 bombs. All four conditions (earth barricades, strong bombs, minimal dunnage, and the 1 m height limit does not apply at D2) are met. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 allows D2 for 30,001–120,000 kg. Compliant.

A large HD 1.1 PES uses effective barricades to reduce the required IMD significantly. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(a) explicitly identifies this as a significant cost factor in depot design and confirms barricades allow reduced IMD values from Table 1. Compliant.

D1 barricaded stack distances are applied between stacks of HD 1.1 bombs. The stacks are separated by concrete walls rather than earth barricades. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 condition 1 specifically requires 'effective earth barricades.' Concrete walls are not earth barricades; the condition is not met. Non-compliant.

An HD 1.1 depot uses barricades to reduce Explosives Workshop Distances. The reduced distances are taken from Annex A Table 1. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(b) confirms barricades allow reduced Explosives Workshop Distances for HD 1.1. Compliant.

HD 1.1 bombs with NEQ 120,000 kg per stack are stored using D2 barricaded distances. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 specifies D2 for up to 120,000 kg — exactly 120,000 kg is within range; however, if the NEQ is even 1 kg above, D2 no longer applies. If the stated 120,000 kg is actually 120,001 kg, D2 would be non-compliant. For scenario purposes the NEQ is 121,000 kg; D2 is non-compliant.

Investigations of accidents and trials data cited in AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(c) are used to justify reduced exterior Q-D with barricades. The reduced distances comply with the recorded data. The standard explicitly permits reduced exterior Q-D supported by accident/trial data. Compliant.

Barricaded stacks of HD 1.1 bombs: earth barricades between stacks, structurally strong bombs, minimal dunnage, D1 distances applied, stacking height exactly 1 m. All four conditions of AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 are satisfied at their limits. Compliant.

A barricaded stack of HD 1.1 bombs uses D1 distances. The stacking height is 1.1 m. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 condition 4 states the stacking height 'must not exceed 1 m' for D1. 1.1 m exceeds the 1 m maximum. Non-compliant.

A depot applies barricade-reduced IMD but only verifies the barricade height, not its extent (length along the sides). An incomplete barricade allows blast and projections to bypass it. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2 refers to 'effective barricades.' A barricade that can be bypassed is not effective; applying reduced distances for an ineffective barricade is non-compliant.
D1 distances are applied for barricaded HD 1.1 bomb stacks with NEQ = 20,000 kg. AASTP-1 1.3.3.1 allows D1 for up to 30,000 kg NEQ. 20,000 kg is within the range. Compliant.

A barricaded PES in an HD 1.1 depot achieves reduced exterior Q-D to an inhabited building by placing an effective barricade between the PES and the IBD boundary. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(c) confirms blast/projection data supports reduced exterior distances with effective barricades. Compliant.

A depot uses a barricade credit for both IMD and exterior IBD simultaneously, but the barricade is only between two internal PES, not between any PES and the inhabited building. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(c) requires the barricade to be between the PES and the ES in question for the credit to apply. A barricade between two internal PES cannot credit the exterior IBD. Non-compliant.

D1 barricaded stack conditions are all met and an explosion in one stack provides a high degree of protection against simultaneous detonation of adjacent stacks, per AASTP-1 1.3.3.1. The design intent and protection level are correctly stated. Compliant.

Functions not directly related to operating requirements or security of ammunition are sited at or beyond the Inhabited Building Distance from the nearest PES. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(a) states that such functions should in principle be sited at or beyond the IBD. The arrangement is compliant.

A large recreational centre with permanent structures is sited inside the IBD of an HD 1.1 PES with no additional protective measures. The centre serves 200 people daily. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(a) states functions not related to operations or security should be sited at or beyond the IBD. A permanent recreational centre for large numbers is non-compliant at less than IBD.

A facility that must be located inside the IBD for operational reasons is provided with additional protection measures and its siting is approved as a necessary operational exception. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(b) acknowledges that some activities will of necessity be inside the IBD. Siting an operationally necessary facility inside IBD with authority approval is compliant.

An administrative office block housing 50 staff is sited at the Inhabited Building Distance from the nearest HD 1.1 PES. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 identifies structures with a considerable number of occupants (more than 20) as acceptable at IBD. An office block with 50 staff at IBD is compliant.

A main depot canteen for 100 personnel is sited at less than the IBD from an HD 1.1 PES, without any additional protection measures. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 lists mess halls and canteens among facilities that should be at IBD. Siting the canteen inside IBD without justification is non-compliant.
A non-explosives workshop is sited at the Inhabited Building Distance from the nearest PES. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 specifically lists non-explosives workshops as acceptable at IBD. Compliant.

Main office buildings housing the depot headquarters staff (30 people) are sited at 60% of the Inhabited Building Distance from an HD 1.1 PES. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 designates main office buildings as appropriate for the IBD level. Siting them at 60% of IBD without additional protection is non-compliant.
Main shower and changing facilities for 50 depot workers are sited at the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 includes main shower and changing facilities in the list acceptable at IBD. Compliant.

A parking area within the IBD zone is classified as a 'temporarily used facility quickly clearable' and accepted below the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7.7 (below IBD) lists parking places as acceptable at a lower protection level. Compliant.
A small arms range without permanent structures is sited at less than the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7 lists small arms ranges as facilities acceptable below the IBD. Compliant.

A recreational centre with permanent structures (covered grandstand, permanent buildings) is sited below the IBD, classified as a 'recreational area.' AASTP-1 1.3.7 permits recreational areas below IBD only 'where no structures are involved.' Permanent structures disqualify this classification. Non-compliant.

A public path running through the depot perimeter is sited at less than IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7 includes public paths among facilities acceptable below IBD as temporarily used facilities that can be quickly cleared. Compliant.

Some operational facilities cannot be sited at the IBD and are accepted inside it, with the nature, type and number of people documented and risk accepted by authority. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(b) acknowledges this necessity. Documented acceptance with authority approval is compliant.

A depot places its security guard posts at the IBD from all PES. The guard posts are directly connected with the security functions of the explosives area. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(a) exempts facilities directly related to the security of ammunition and explosives from the IBD requirement — guard posts may be sited closer. Placing security posts at IBD is not non-compliant per se, but requiring security functions at IBD when the standard permits them closer is an unnecessarily restrictive misapplication.

A main canteen serving 80 depot personnel is sited at the IBD. AASTP-1 identifies mess halls and main canteens as the types of structure with considerable numbers of occupants (>20) acceptable at IBD. Compliant.

A permanent sports pavilion accommodating 150 spectators is sited below the IBD, justified as a recreational area. AASTP-1 permits recreational areas below IBD only when no structures are involved. A pavilion with 150 occupants is a permanent structure. Non-compliant.

An unbarricaded stack of ammunition is considered when defining the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 specifically includes unbarricaded stacks of ammunition in the list of items/facilities for which IBD provides the appropriate protection level. Compliant.

A depot plan treats all functions — including headquarters office, canteen, and sports facilities — as exempt from IBD because they are 'depot support functions.' AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(a) requires functions not directly related to operations or security to be sited at IBD. Administrative and welfare functions are not exempt. Non-compliant.
A railways line of minor importance passes within the zone inside the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7 includes railways of minor to medium importance as acceptable below IBD. Compliant.

A major national railway line is routed through the zone below IBD. AASTP-1 allows railways 'of minor to medium importance' below IBD. A major national railway is not of minor to medium importance. Non-compliant.

A training school for 150 students with permanent classrooms is sited at less than IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 lists structures with considerable numbers of occupants (>20) as appropriate for the IBD level, not below it. A school with 150 students must be at IBD. Non-compliant.

Main office buildings at a depot ammunition facility are sited exactly at the IBD. AASTP-1 1.3.7.4 identifies main office buildings as the class of facility for which IBD provides acceptable protection. Siting them at IBD is correct. Compliant.

A security checkpoint at a depot gate is sited inside the IBD because it is directly related to the security of the explosives area. AASTP-1 1.3.7.1(a) permits facilities directly connected with security of ammunition to be sited inside the IBD. Compliant.

A navigable waterway of minor importance passes 10% inside the IBD zone. AASTP-1 lists navigable waterways of minor to medium importance as acceptable below IBD. A minor waterway qualifies. Compliant.

A permanently staffed fire station with 20 firefighters is sited at less than IBD, justified as a 'temporarily occupied facility.' A permanently staffed fire station is not a facility that 'assembles people only temporarily and can be quickly cleared.' It has continuous occupancy. Non-compliant.

A main public traffic route (dense traffic) passes 400 m from an HD 1.1 PES that is a heavy-walled building. AASTP-1 states 400 m is tolerable for main PTR with dense traffic when the PES is a heavy-walled or earth-covered building. Compliant.

A main public traffic route (dense traffic) passes 270 m from an HD 1.1 PES that is an open stack. AASTP-1 states 270 m is tolerable for main PTR/dense traffic only when the PES is an open stack or light structure. However, 270 m for dense traffic with an open stack is listed under 270 m — so actually 270m IS listed for main PTR/dense traffic/open stack. This is UYGUN: 270 m is explicitly tolerable for main PTR dense traffic with open stack.

A main public traffic route with dense traffic passes 270 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 states 270 m is tolerable for main PTR/dense traffic when PES is open stack or light structure — NOT when the PES is heavy-walled. For a heavy-walled PES, the 270 m distance does not appear in the tolerable list. Non-compliant: 400 m is required.

A public traffic route (non-dense traffic) is 270 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 lists 270 m as tolerable for 'public traffic routes when traffic is not dense and the PES is a heavy-walled or earth-covered building.' Compliant.

A public traffic route (non-dense traffic) is 200 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 specifies 270 m as the tolerable distance for non-dense traffic with heavy-walled PES. 200 m is below 270 m. Non-compliant.

A sparsely populated area is 270 m from an open-stack PES. AASTP-1 states 270 m is tolerable for sparsely populated areas when the PES is an open stack or light structure, noting a small expectation of damage or injury from projections. Compliant.

A built-up residential area is 270 m from an open-stack PES. AASTP-1 specifies 270 m for sparsely populated areas with open stack — not built-up areas. For built-up areas, 400 m is required (with open stack). Non-compliant.
A built-up area is 400 m from an open-stack PES. AASTP-1 states 400 m is tolerable for built-up areas when the PES is an open stack or light structure. Compliant.
A built-up area is 350 m from an open-stack PES. AASTP-1 requires 400 m for built-up areas with open stacks. 350 m is non-compliant.
All inhabited buildings are 400 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 states 400 m is tolerable for all inhabited buildings when the PES is heavy-walled or earth-covered. Compliant.
All inhabited buildings are 350 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 requires 400 m for inhabited buildings when PES is heavy-walled. 350 m is below the required 400 m. Non-compliant.
A main PTR with dense traffic is 400 m from an earth-covered PES. AASTP-1 specifies 400 m is tolerable for main PTR (dense traffic) with heavy-walled or earth-covered PES. Compliant.

Inhabited buildings are 270 m from an open-stack PES. AASTP-1 lists 270 m for sparsely populated areas with open stacks, not for inhabited buildings. For inhabited buildings with an open stack, 400 m is required. Non-compliant.

A public road (minor to medium importance) runs inside the IBD zone. AASTP-1 1.3.7 includes roads and navigable waterways of minor to medium importance as acceptable below IBD. The road qualifies. Compliant.

A major intercity highway is routed within the IBD zone. AASTP-1 permits roads of 'minor to medium importance' below IBD, with risk mitigation noted (road sides free from obstacles). A major intercity highway is not minor to medium importance. Non-compliant.

A public road inside the IBD has its road sides cleared of obstacles likely to injure vehicle occupants if a driver reacts to an explosion. AASTP-1 notes that for public roads below IBD, the risk of secondary injury can be reduced by ensuring road sides are free from obstacles. This mitigation is applied. Compliant.

A minor public road inside the IBD zone is lined with concrete barriers on both sides. AASTP-1 notes that obstacles on road sides increase the risk of secondary injury to drivers reacting to an explosion. Concrete barriers are exactly the type of obstacle the standard warns against. Non-compliant.

A sparsely populated rural area is 270 m from a light-structure PES. The safety plan acknowledges a small expectation of damage or injury from projections. AASTP-1 states 270 m for sparsely populated areas with light structure PES is tolerable with this acknowledged small risk. Compliant.

A depot safety plan applies 270 m to all external areas including built-up areas, claiming this is sufficient for any land use category. AASTP-1 differentiates by land use: built-up areas require 400 m from an open stack. Applying 270 m universally regardless of land use type is non-compliant.
An earth-covered PES has an inhabited building at 400 m. AASTP-1 specifies 400 m is tolerable for all inhabited buildings when PES is earth-covered. Compliant.

A light-structure PES has inhabited buildings 300 m away. AASTP-1 requires 400 m for inhabited buildings with a light-structure PES (listed under 'all inhabited buildings when PES is heavy-walled or earth-covered' — light structure does not qualify for the 400 m inhabited building provision). Non-compliant.
A parking area inside the IBD is used by depot vehicles only and can be quickly cleared. AASTP-1 lists parking places as acceptable below IBD as temporarily used facilities. Compliant.
A non-dense traffic public road is 270 m from a heavy-walled PES. AASTP-1 states 270 m is tolerable for non-dense traffic with heavy-walled or earth-covered PES. Compliant.
A non-dense traffic road is 200 m from an earth-covered PES. AASTP-1 requires 270 m for non-dense traffic with heavy-walled or earth-covered PES. 200 m is below the required 270 m. Non-compliant.

A depot's projection hazard assessment applies 270 m to non-dense traffic routes adjacent to a heavy-walled PES and 400 m to main/dense traffic routes and inhabited buildings adjacent to the same PES. Both distances are correctly applied per AASTP-1. Compliant.

An underground magazine is designed using underground storage criteria. The minimum distance from the perimeter of the storage area to any external surface is 900 mm and exceeds 0.1 × Q^(1/3) for the given NEQ. AASTP-1 requires both conditions to be met before underground criteria apply; both are satisfied. Compliant.

An underground storage facility uses underground Q-D criteria. The minimum cover to any external surface is 400 mm. AASTP-1 requires the minimum distance to exceed 600 mm (and 0.1 × Q^(1/3)); 400 mm is below 600 mm. Aboveground criteria must be used. Non-compliant.

An underground facility with 700 mm cover uses underground criteria. The NEQ is 8,000 kg, giving 0.1 × Q^(1/3) = 0.1 × 20 = 2.0 m. The cover of 700 mm is less than 2.0 m. AASTP-1 requires BOTH 600 mm AND 0.1 × Q^(1/3) to be exceeded. With cover < 2.0 m, underground criteria do not apply. Non-compliant.

An underground storage facility has 800 mm cover and a NEQ of 1,000 kg (0.1 × Q^(1/3) = 0.1 × 10 = 1.0 m). The cover of 800 mm exceeds 600 mm but does not exceed 1.0 m. Aboveground criteria are applied. AASTP-1 requires cover > 600 mm AND > 0.1 × Q^(1/3); since 800 mm < 1,000 mm, aboveground criteria are correctly applied. Compliant.

An underground facility uses properly designed and closed blast doors to separate different storage chambers, preventing long-duration blast effects from spreading throughout the complex. AASTP-1 1.3 (underground) states that unless robust engineered closing devices are used and kept closed, blast effects spread throughout the entire complex. Using closed blast doors as specified is compliant.

An underground facility relies on its chamber layout alone — without blast doors — to prevent blast spread. AASTP-1 states that without robust engineered closing devices, long-duration blast effects spread throughout the entire underground complex. Layout alone is insufficient; blast doors are required. Non-compliant.

Blast doors in an underground facility are designed to withstand the internal pressure from an accidental explosion in an adjacent chamber. They are inspected regularly to ensure they remain closed when not in use. AASTP-1 requires doors to be 'properly designed' and 'closed' to provide separation. Compliant.

An underground magazine has blast doors but they are routinely left open during working hours for access convenience. AASTP-1 states that closing devices must be closed to provide the desired separation; open doors provide no blast separation. Non-compliant.

Q-D distances for an underground chamber storing HD 1.1, 1.3, 1.5 and 1.6 are determined from the total quantity in the chamber. No subdivision prevents rapid communication between areas. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(a) requires total quantity when no subdivision prevents rapid communication. Using total quantity is compliant.

An underground complex stores HD 1.1 in connected chambers without effective subdivision. Q-D is computed per-chamber rather than for the total connected volume. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(b) requires a connected chamber/cavern site to be treated as a single chamber unless explosion communication is prevented. Per-chamber computation is non-compliant.

An underground facility stores HD 1.3 propellants. The Q-D is determined as if the material were HD 1.1. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(c) explicitly states that HD 1.3 material should be treated as HD 1.1 when stored underground. Compliant.

An underground facility stores HD 1.3 propellants. The Q-D is computed using HD 1.3 parameters, which gives shorter distances. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(c) requires underground HD 1.3 to be treated as HD 1.1. Using HD 1.3 parameters for underground storage is non-compliant.

Two underground chambers are separated by an effective explosion-resistant subdivision wall. Q-D is computed per chamber rather than for the combined total. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(b) permits treating chambers separately if explosion communication is prevented by adequate subdivision. Compliant.

An underground complex claims adequate subdivision between its two HD 1.1 chambers based on a standard concrete wall. Q-D is computed per-chamber. AASTP-1 requires 'adequate subdivision or chamber separation' to prevent explosion communication; a standard concrete wall without engineering analysis may not qualify. Without documented effectiveness, treating chambers separately is non-compliant.

An underground chamber of a natural cavern has its perimeter-to-surface distance verified to exceed both 600 mm and 0.1 × Q^(1/3). Underground Q-D criteria are applied. AASTP-1 conditions for underground criteria are both met. Compliant.

An underground HD 1.2/1.4 store is designed assuming an initial event will start a fire rather than a detonation. Accordingly, fire-suppression provisions are prioritised. AASTP-1 notes that HD 1.2 and 1.4 initial events in underground storage usually start a fire, not a detonation. Designing fire-suppression accordingly is compliant.

An underground HD 1.2 store assumes all accidental events will be detonations and sizes blast containment accordingly, with no fire suppression provisions. AASTP-1 states HD 1.2 and 1.4 underground events usually start a fire. Ignoring fire suppression in favour of blast-only design is non-compliant.

A shallow tunnel with 500 mm cover over the perimeter of the storage area applies underground Q-D criteria. AASTP-1 requires minimum distance > 600 mm to the external surface; 500 mm fails this requirement. Aboveground criteria must be used. Non-compliant.

An underground complex storing HD 1.1 and HD 1.5 in separate chambers with an effective explosion-resistant partition computes Q-D per chamber. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1 permits per-chamber Q-D when explosion communication is prevented. Compliant.

An underground facility stores HD 1.3 and HD 1.1 in connected chambers without separation and computes Q-D using HD 1.3 parameters for the entire volume. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1: HD 1.3 underground must be treated as HD 1.1, and connected chambers without separation are treated as a single site. Using HD 1.3 parameters for connected undivided chambers is doubly non-compliant.

An underground storage facility is a natural cavern with geological parameters confirmed by site investigation. Q-D is based on site-specific geology per AASTP-1 underground chapter. AASTP-1 acknowledges that ground shock parameters vary significantly and require site-specific investigation. Using confirmed site geology is compliant.

An underground facility's blast doors are designed for personnel loading only, not blast. When not in use they remain open. AASTP-1 requires robust engineered closing devices designed for blast loads and kept closed; standard personnel doors do not qualify. Non-compliant.

Q-D for an underground HD 1.5 site is determined from the total explosive quantity using HD 1.1 parameters. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1 requires HD 1.5 distances from total quantity, and HD 1.5 is listed alongside HD 1.1. Compliant.

An underground facility subdivides HD 1.1 into 10 small chambers and computes Q-D from each chamber's NEQ independently, without verifying that the subdivision prevents rapid communication of an incident. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1(a) requires subdivision to prevent rapid communication before per-chamber Q-D is used. Without verified effective subdivision, total quantity must be used. Non-compliant.

An explosives workshop is sited at the distance specified in AASTP-1 for Explosives Workshop Distances from the nearest PES. The distance provides reasonable immunity for personnel from blast, flame, radiant heat and projections. Compliant.

An explosives workshop is sited at 80% of the minimum Explosives Workshop Distance. The depot argues that the workshop has reinforced walls. AASTP-1 1.3.4 defines Explosives Workshop Distances as minimum permissible; unilateral reduction based on wall reinforcement is not permitted without authority approval. Non-compliant.

The safety plan for an explosives workshop notes that light structures are likely to be severely damaged or destroyed at the Explosives Workshop Distance. Personnel in the workshop are considered acceptably protected at this distance. AASTP-1 1.3.4 explicitly acknowledges this trade-off; the plan correctly characterises the protection level. Compliant.

An explosives workshop is described in the safety plan as 'fully protected' against blast and destruction at the Explosives Workshop Distance. AASTP-1 states the workshop distance provides 'reasonable degree of immunity' but light structures are 'likely to be severely damaged, if not completely destroyed.' Claiming 'full protection' misrepresents the standard. Non-compliant.

A storage building is shared for HD 1.2 and HD 1.3 at different times as storage requirements vary. The applicable distances are recalculated each time the hazard division changes. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(b) acknowledges that buildings may be used for different hazard divisions over time. Recalculating distances appropriately is compliant.

A building used for HD 1.3 storage is later repurposed for HD 1.1 without revising the Q-D calculations. The distances were computed for HD 1.3 only. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(b) notes buildings may store different hazard divisions over time, implying Q-D must be appropriate for the current hazard division. Failing to revise distances when switching to HD 1.1 is non-compliant.

Q-D distances for a storage area are set based on the Table 1-3 values for explosives safety. Structural requirements and fire-fighting access are handled by additional analyses. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(c) states the table distances are based on explosives safety and do not account for structural or fire-fighting access requirements. Addressing them separately is the correct approach. Compliant.

A building's footprint is expanded to include a fire vehicle access road, and the Q-D distance is measured from the expanded footprint instead of the explosive store perimeter. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(c) notes fire access may require greater distances than the tables, but Q-D is measured from the PES (explosive content), not from fire access structures. Incorrect Q-D measurement origin is non-compliant.

Propulsive rockets (unpackaged, assembled) are stored in a special building designed to prevent accidental flight. The Q-D for the appropriate hazard division is also applied. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 requires both: special storage to prevent flight AND hazard division Q-D. Meeting both conditions is compliant.

Propulsive rockets in assembled condition are stored in a standard igloo without devices to prevent flight. The depot applies only the standard HD 1.3 Q-D. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 states unpackaged propulsive rockets must be in special buildings or held by devices to prevent flight. Standard igloo without flight-prevention devices is non-compliant.

Packaged rockets in storage and transport configuration are stored in a standard magazine with standard Q-D for their hazard division. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 states rockets in assembled or unassembled condition when packaged as for storage/transport do not present special problems and standard Q-D applies. Compliant.

Assembled propulsive rockets held on a tactical launcher inside a depot are assessed using only standard Q-D for HD 1.3, without any flight-prevention constraint. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 requires special buildings or flight-prevention devices for propulsive rockets in the assembled condition. Standard Q-D alone is insufficient. Non-compliant.

An explosives workshop distance provides a high degree of protection against immediate or subsequent propagation of explosion from the adjacent PES. AASTP-1 1.3.4 explicitly states the Explosives Workshop Distance provides this protection. The design intent is correctly stated. Compliant.

A storage building is not designed to withstand direct attack by hostile activities. Only explosives safety Q-D distances are used; structural requirements are addressed separately. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(a) states it is not practicable to construct surface buildings to withstand direct hostile attack. The design approach is correctly scoped. Compliant.

An explosives workshop is sited at the correct distance but personnel inside are told it provides complete protection against all effects of a nearby explosion. AASTP-1 1.3.4 states the distance provides 'reasonable degree of immunity' — not complete protection. Describing it as complete protection is incorrect and non-compliant.

Rockets on launchers at a missile installation are assessed per Part IV, Chapter 3, as a special case of the AASTP-1. Standard Q-D rules are not applied to this configuration. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 explicitly designates missiles on launchers at missile installations as a special case under Part IV. Applying the special case rules is compliant.

A storage building plan applies only the structural requirements from civil engineering standards, ignoring AASTP-1 explosives safety distances on the basis that structural calculations already ensure safety. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(c) states the table distances are based on explosives safety, separate from structural requirements. Both must be satisfied. Non-compliant.

An explosives workshop at the correct minimum distance provides personnel with reasonable immunity from blast, flame, radiant heat and projections from a nearby PES explosion. The design accepts that the light structure may be severely damaged but personnel survivability is maintained at the EWD. AASTP-1 1.3.4 confirms this trade-off. Compliant.

Unpackaged propulsive rockets stored in an open storage area are assessed only against flight range requirements and no Q-D for the hazard division is applied. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 requires both: flight prevention AND hazard division Q-D. Applying only flight range without hazard division Q-D is non-compliant.

A storage building is changed from HD 1.2 to HD 1.1 use. Q-D are fully recalculated for HD 1.1. Access roads for fire-fighting are confirmed to remain clear. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(b-c) requires recalculation and noting fire access needs. Compliant.
The minimum Explosives Workshop Distance is measured from the nearest PES (the ammunition store) to the workshop. This is the correct measurement reference per AASTP-1 1.3.4. Compliant.

An explosives workshop is sited at the minimum Explosives Workshop Distance from the nearest inhabited building rather than from the nearest PES. AASTP-1 1.3.4 defines EWD as the minimum distance from PES to workshop. Measuring from an inhabited building instead of the PES is incorrect. Non-compliant.

Missiles in their packaged transport/storage condition are stored in a standard magazine with standard Q-D. AASTP-1 1.3.3.5 states packaged rockets present no special flight problem; standard Q-D for the appropriate hazard division is sufficient. Compliant.

A building stores HD 1.2 ammunition. A decision is made not to include road space for fire-fighting access in the site plan to save land. AASTP-1 1.3.5.1(c) explicitly notes that practical considerations such as fire-fighting access may require greater distances than the tables. Omitting fire access planning is non-compliant with the standard's guidance.

An explosives workshop provides a high degree of protection against propagation of explosion by being sited at the minimum Explosives Workshop Distance from the nearest PES. AASTP-1 1.3.4 confirms this protection level at the EWD. Compliant.

An HD 1.1 depot applies barricaded Explosives Workshop Distances from Annex A Table 1. Barricades are in place between each PES and the relevant workshop. AASTP-1 1.3.6.2(b) explicitly states that an effective barricade around a workshop reduces the required Explosives Workshop Distance. Applying the reduced distances from Table 1 with effective barricades in place is compliant.

An IMD assessment for a new ammunition store applies the same standard distance to both a primary explosive store and a conventional artillery shell store, ignoring the difference in sensitiveness. AASTP-1 1.3.1.4 states that primary explosives require individual assessment and the standard table IMD must not be applied to them. Using the same table distance for primary and secondary explosives is non-compliant.

An underground facility with HD 1.6N material applies Q-D distances derived from the total quantity in connected chambers without effective explosion-preventing subdivision. AASTP-1 1.4.2.1 requires total quantity when no subdivision prevents rapid communication; HD 1.6N is covered by the underground criteria. Using total quantity for connected chambers is compliant.
